comment_text.sn7.9:1.1: This Sutta, and its expanded parallel at Snp 3.4, contains a dense array of allusions and plays on Vedic concepts.
comment_text.sn7.9:1.2: This is evidently the midday fire oblation described at Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa 2.2.3. A brahmin householder, worshiping when the sun is at its most powerful and the forces of darkness weakest, renders offerings to Agni so that he will drive away evils. | The Sundarikā was a sacred river (MN 7:20.3). Bathing in the ford there was said to bring beauty (Mahābhārata 3.82.51).
comment_text.sn7.9:1.4: The concept of “leftovers” (sesa) is supremely important in the ritual, for what is burned away is for the gods and humans live on what is left. Normally leavings would have been given to a brahmin and not lower castes (Manu 4.80, 3.249; Khādira Gṛhyasūtra 2.1.29). | At Snp 3.4:10.5 the offering is specified as a sacrificial cake (pūraḷāsa, Sanskrit puroḍāśa).
comment_text.sn7.9:2.2: From here the Sutta omits a passage from Snp 3.4:3.1–14.1.
comment_text.sn7.9:3.1: Here we rejoin Snp 3.4:14.1 for a time.
comment_text.sn7.9:3.2: The line plays with jāti (“birth”), jāyati (“give birth”), and jātaveda. The latter is a Vedic word for fire. Its meaning is obscure, but I think the sense is jāta (“those who have been born”, i.e. “mortals”) plus vedas (“knowledge”), i.e. “one who brings knowledge to mortals”. This is the fire-god Agni, who comes down from the sun to the sacrificial fire, bringing light and awareness and Vedic knowledge to those who have been born, namely mortal humans. In this passage, it implies that anyone who “has been born” can “give birth” to “knowledge” regardless of their “birth”.
comment_text.sn7.9:4.2: “Complete knowledge master” is vedantagū, which in the Vedic system would mean, “one who has completely mastered the Vedas”.
comment_text.sn7.9:4.3: Upavhayati occurs only here and DN 20:11.15 in Pali. It is  Vedic term for invoking or calling up blessings in a whisper. | From here the text diverges again from Snp 3.4:15.3, which inserts a long series of verses.
comment_text.sn7.9:6.2: Chāndogya Upaniṣad 5.24.4 says that one performing the fire ritual with true wisdom may even offer to an outcaste.
comment_text.sn7.9:9.2: At Snp 3.4:34.4 the Buddha recommends giving instead to arahants.
comment_text.sn7.9:9.3: In a text on the agnihotra, Prajāpatī is depicted as pouring the oblation on plants and water (Kāṭhaka Saṃhitā 6.2). The Āpastamba Dharmasūtra says that the student must “not leave any food uneaten. If he is unable to do so, he should bury the leftovers in the ground, (or) throw them in the water” (1.4.12). See too Śathapatha Brāhmaṇa 3.8.5.9, where the stake for the heart of the sacrificial beast is disposed on the muddy ground between water and dry land, so as to disturb neither.
comment_text.sn7.9:11.2: In these verses, the Buddha systematically critiques and realigns the fire sacrifice.
comment_text.sn7.9:13.3: This passage plays on the homonyms samādahati (“kindle”, root meaning “to burn”) and samādahati (“serene”, “stilled”, “immersed”, root meaning “to place”). He no longer “burns” the sacred flame, but “is serene” with the inner light of samādhi.
comment_text.sn7.9:14.1: Such passages of equivalences or connections are characteristic of post-Vedic literature. For example, Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa 11.5.6 says the juhū spoon is speech, the upabhṛt cup  the mind, the dhruvā vessel the eye, the sruvā ladle the mental power, the bath the truth, the conclusion heaven. | A “pack” (khāri_) was originally “donkey-load”, but here it must be a heavy weight for a single person. The Buddha is saying that carrying around the cenceit of “self” is a heavy burden.
comment_text.sn7.9:14.2: Smoke conveys the sacrifice to heaven (eg. Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa 3.7.133, “May smoke rise to the sky, light to heaven, may ash fill the earth). For smoke in the sense of “fuming”, see AN 4.200:13.1, MN 23:2.1. | Sacred ash, the purifying divine residue from the fire, was smeared on the forehead or body as a sign of holiness. Here, however, the implication is that when the “fuming” anger is cold, all that is left are the lies spoken in the heat of rage. The line plays with the curse that those who hate us should be reduced to ashes (Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa 6.6.3.10, see MN 93:18.19).
comment_text.sn7.9:14.3: The sujā (Sanskrit sruvā) is a small ladle of udumbara fig wood. It is dipped into the ghee or milk, lapping like a tongue, a metaphor often deployed in the Rig Veda (1.61.5, 3.31.3, 5.1.3, 10.109.5). The sacrifice of ghee, honey, and milk is driven by the craving to experience such delicious tastes. | The jotiṭhāna (“fireplace”) was a depression in the ground in which the fire was kindled. Here joti must be translated “fire”, but it is the same word as “light” in the next line. The Buddha is saying that the light/fire is kindled in the heart (hadayaṁ jotiṭhānaṁ). This draws on Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 4.3.7, where the Self is the “light within the heart” (hṛdyantarjyoti).
comment_text.sn7.9:14.4: Attā … purisassa joti is a direct reference to Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 4.3.2. Janaka asks Yājñavalkya, “What is this person’s light?” (kiṃjyotirayaṃ puruṣa). Yājñavalkya answers, “The self, indeed, is his light” (ātmaivāsya jyotirbhavati). The key difference is the term “well-tamed” (sudanto). For the Buddha, what is known as a “self” is conditioned and changing, so must be developed, and only then becomes a blessing. In such cases, “self” is practically equivalent to “mind”.
comment_text.sn7.9:15.1: The lake (rahada) was a pool of water into which the sacrificer descends seeking renewal or protection (Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa 4.1.5.12, 4.4.5.10). | The tittha was the sacred bathing place, originally the stairs or descent that provided passage to the waters. | This verse occurs independently at SN 7.21:3.1.
comment_text.sn7.9:15.4: The use of deliberate paradox—bathe without getting wet—is characteristic of Vedic verse. The water rolls off them as it does a lotus.
comment_text.sn7.9:16.1: See similar sets of virtues at DN 30:1.6.2 and SN 24.6:1.7. | This verse seems clumsy to me, and I retain that in the translation.
comment_text.sn7.9:16.2: “Attainment of divinity” (brahmapatti) is found in Brahmanical sense at Katha Upaniṣad 6.18.
comment_text.sn7.9:16.3: The commentary says the t in sat is a mere particle, and the pronoun should be read as second person. | The “upright ones” are the arahants.
comment_text.sn7.9:16.4: Dhammasārī is a unique phrase. Normally, sārī means “one who follows”, making this an abbreviation of dhammānusārī, a “follower of teachings”. Elsewhere, however, this refers only to a specific stage prior to stream-entry (SN 25.1:2.1). We do find dhammasāra elsewhere in the sense “heartwood of the teaching” (SN 55.43:5.3, Thag 16.3:18.2), and perhaps sārī here means “having the essence of”. This form is not attested in Pali, but it is found in Sanskrit. Such an option is not endorsed by the commentary, which supplies two possibilities. One relies on the causative form sāretvā, “one who makes unskilful qualities leave”. The second is curious: one is “covered by the sārī of Dhamma”. That this means the saree dress is confirmed by the subcommentary’s “dressed and decorated”. If correct, this may be the earliest occurrence of sārī in this sense; the standard Pali spelling is sāṭī. By this reading we might translate, “draped in the teaching”. | “That man” according to the commentary refers to one who practices in such a way.

root_text.sn7.9:1.1: Ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā kosalesu viharati sundarikāya nadiyā tīre.
root_text.sn7.9:1.2: Tena kho pana samayena sundarikabhāradvājo brāhmaṇo sundarikāya nadiyā tīre aggiṁ juhati, aggihuttaṁ paricarati.
root_text.sn7.9:1.3: Atha kho sundarikabhāradvājo brāhmaṇo aggiṁ juhitvā aggihuttaṁ paricaritvā uṭṭhāyāsanā samantā catuddisā anuvilokesi:
root_text.sn7.9:1.5: Addasā kho sundarikabhāradvājo brāhmaṇo bhagavantaṁ aññatarasmiṁ rukkhamūle sasīsaṁ pārutaṁ nisinnaṁ.
root_text.sn7.9:1.6: Disvāna vāmena hatthena habyasesaṁ gahetvā dakkhiṇena hatthena kamaṇḍaluṁ gahetvā yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami.
root_text.sn7.9:1.7: Atha kho bhagavā sundarikabhāradvājassa brāhmaṇassa padasaddena sīsaṁ vivari.
root_text.sn7.9:1.8: Atha kho sundarikabhāradvājo brāhmaṇo “muṇḍo ayaṁ bhavaṁ, muṇḍako ayaṁ bhavan”ti tatova puna nivattitukāmo ahosi.
root_text.sn7.9:2.1: Atha kho sundarikabhāradvājo brāhmaṇo yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn7.9:9.1: “Atha kassa cāhaṁ, bho gotama, imaṁ habyasesaṁ dammī”ti?
root_text.sn7.9:9.2: “Na khvāhaṁ, brāhmaṇa, passāmi sadevake loke samārake sabrahmake sassamaṇabrāhmaṇiyā pajāya sadevamanussāya yasseso habyaseso bhutto sammā pariṇāmaṁ gaccheyya aññatra, brāhmaṇa, tathāgatassa vā tathāgatasāvakassa vā.
root_text.sn7.9:9.3: Tena hi tvaṁ, brāhmaṇa, taṁ habyasesaṁ appaharite vā chaḍḍehi appāṇake vā udake opilāpehī”ti.
root_text.sn7.9:10.1: Atha kho sundarikabhāradvājo brāhmaṇo taṁ habyasesaṁ appāṇake udake opilāpesi.
root_text.sn7.9:10.2: Atha kho so habyaseso udake pakkhitto cicciṭāyati ciṭiciṭāyati sandhūpāyati sampadhūpāyati.
root_text.sn7.9:10.3: Seyyathāpi nāma phālo divasaṁsantatto udake pakkhitto cicciṭāyati ciṭiciṭāyati sandhūpāyati sampadhūpāyati;
root_text.sn7.9:10.4: evameva so habyaseso udake pakkhitto cicciṭāyati ciṭiciṭāyati sandhūpāyati sampadhūpāyati.
root_text.sn7.9:11.1: Atha kho sundarikabhāradvājo brāhmaṇo saṁviggo lomahaṭṭhajāto yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā ekamantaṁ aṭṭhāsi.
root_text.sn7.9:11.2: Ekamantaṁ ṭhitaṁ kho sundarikabhāradvājaṁ brāhmaṇaṁ bhagavā gāthāhi ajjhabhāsi:
root_text.sn7.9:12.3: Na hi tena suddhiṁ kusalā vadanti,
root_text.sn7.9:17.1: Evaṁ vutte, sundarikabhāradvājo brāhmaṇo bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn7.9:17.3: aññataro ca panāyasmā bhāradvājo arahataṁ ahosīti.

translation_text.sn7.9:1.1: At one time the Buddha was staying in the Kosalan lands on the bank of the Sundarikā river.
translation_text.sn7.9:1.2: Now at that time the brahmin Bhāradvāja of Sundarikā was serving the sacred flame and performing the fire sacrifice on the bank of the Sundarikā.
translation_text.sn7.9:1.3: Then he surveyed the four quarters, wondering,
translation_text.sn7.9:1.4: “Now who might eat the leftovers of this oblation?”
translation_text.sn7.9:1.5: He saw the Buddha meditating at the root of a certain tree with his robe pulled over his head.
translation_text.sn7.9:1.6: Taking the leftovers of the oblation in his left hand and a pitcher in the right he approached the Buddha.
translation_text.sn7.9:1.7: When he heard Bhāradvāja of Sundarikā’s footsteps the Buddha uncovered his head.
translation_text.sn7.9:1.8: Bhāradvāja of Sundarikā thought, “This man is shaven, he is shaven!” And he wanted to turn back.
translation_text.sn7.9:1.11: Why don’t I go to him and ask about his birth?”
translation_text.sn7.9:2.1: Then the brahmin Bhāradvāja of Sundarikā went up to the Buddha, and said to him,
translation_text.sn7.9:3.1: “Don’t ask about birth, ask about conduct;
translation_text.sn7.9:3.2: for the fire of Jātaveda is born from sticks.
translation_text.sn7.9:3.3: A steadfast sage, even though from a low class family,
translation_text.sn7.9:3.4: is a thoroughbred checked by conscience.
translation_text.sn7.9:4.1: Tamed by truth, fulfilled by taming,
translation_text.sn7.9:4.2: a complete knowledge master <j>who has completed the spiritual journey—
translation_text.sn7.9:4.3: that’s who a sacrificer should invite,
translation_text.sn7.9:4.4: and offer a timely oblation <j>to one worthy of a religious donation.”
translation_text.sn7.9:5.1: “My sacrificial offering <j>must have been well performed,
translation_text.sn7.9:5.2: since I have met such a knowledge master!
translation_text.sn7.9:5.3: It’s because I’d never met anyone like you
translation_text.sn7.9:5.4: that others ate the leftover oblation.
translation_text.sn7.9:6.2: The worthy one is a brahmin.”
translation_text.sn7.9:7.1: “Food enchanted by a verse isn’t fit for me to eat.
translation_text.sn7.9:7.2: That’s not the principle of those who see, brahmin.
translation_text.sn7.9:7.3: The Buddhas cast aside things enchanted with verses.
translation_text.sn7.9:7.4: Since there is such a principle, brahmin, <j>that’s how they live.
translation_text.sn7.9:8.1: Serve with other food and drink
translation_text.sn7.9:8.2: the consummate one, the great seer,
translation_text.sn7.9:8.3: with defilements ended and remorse stilled.
translation_text.sn7.9:8.4: For he is the field for the seeker of merit.”
translation_text.sn7.9:9.1: “Then, Mister Gotama, to whom should I give the leftovers of this oblation?”
translation_text.sn7.9:9.2: “Brahmin, I don’t see anyone in this world—with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans—who can properly digest these leftovers, except for the Realized One or one of his disciples.
translation_text.sn7.9:9.3: Well then, brahmin, throw out those leftovers where there is little that grows, or drop them into water that has no living creatures.”
translation_text.sn7.9:10.1: So Bhāradvāja of Sundarikā dropped the leftover oblation in water that had no living creatures.
translation_text.sn7.9:10.2: And when those leftovers were placed in the water, they sizzled and hissed, steaming and fuming.
translation_text.sn7.9:10.3: Suppose there was an iron cauldron that had been heated all day. If you placed it in the water, it would sizzle and hiss, steaming and fuming.
translation_text.sn7.9:10.4: In the same way, when those leftovers were placed in the water, they sizzled and hissed, steaming and fuming.
translation_text.sn7.9:11.1: Then the brahmin Bhāradvāja of Sundarikā, shocked and awestruck, went up to the Buddha, and stood to one side.
translation_text.sn7.9:11.2: The Buddha addressed him in verse:
translation_text.sn7.9:12.2: don’t imagine this is purity, for it’s just external.
translation_text.sn7.9:12.3: For those who are skilled say this is no way to purity,
translation_text.sn7.9:12.4: when one seeks purity in externals.
translation_text.sn7.9:13.1: I’ve given up kindling firewood, brahmin,
translation_text.sn7.9:13.2: now I light only the inner flame.
translation_text.sn7.9:13.4: I am a perfected one leading the spiritual life.
translation_text.sn7.9:14.1: Conceit, brahmin, is the burden of your pack,
translation_text.sn7.9:14.2: anger your smoke, and lies your ashes.
translation_text.sn7.9:14.3: The tongue is the ladle, the heart the fireplace;
translation_text.sn7.9:14.4: a well-tamed self is a person’s light.
translation_text.sn7.9:15.1: The teaching is a lake, brahmin, <j>into which ethics are the stairs,
translation_text.sn7.9:15.2: unclouded, praised by the fine to the good.
translation_text.sn7.9:15.3: There the knowledge masters go to bathe,
translation_text.sn7.9:15.4: and cross to the far shore with dry limbs.
translation_text.sn7.9:16.1: Truth, principle, restraint, the spiritual life,
translation_text.sn7.9:16.2: attaining divinity based on the middle, brahmin—
translation_text.sn7.9:16.3: pay homage to the upright ones.
translation_text.sn7.9:16.4: I declare that man one who follows the teaching.”
translation_text.sn7.9:17.1: When he had spoken, the brahmin Bhāradvāja of Sundarikā said to the Buddha,
translation_text.sn7.9:17.3: And Venerable Bhāradvāja became one of the perfected.

variant_text.sn7.9:10.3: phālo → loho (mr) | divasaṁsantatto → divasasantatto (bj, sya-all, km, pts1ed, pts2ed)
variant_text.sn7.9:13.2: Ajjhattamevujjalayāmi → ajjhattameva jalayāmi (bj, sya-all, km, pts1ed)
